category_summary:
**AI for Science** dominated the day's most valuable discussions. **OpenAI** launched **LifeSciBench**, [a 750-task benchmark](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-a51d19016026) co-built with 173 scientists, and touted **GPT-5.4** [driving a medicinal chemistry project](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-d7886cbb00ee) to a validated experimental result. **Greg Brockman** amplified the chemistry and [AI-in-medicine stories](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-4e947ad4d710).

- **Gary Marcus** sustained the **AGI skepticism** thread, arguing LLMs are [not reliable enough to be 'sound'](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-e59c5cbc65b0) (tying it to Trump's non-circumventable systems request) and that **Mark Zuckerberg** [cannot reach AGI through data labeling](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-90ecd8497d3e) alone.
- **Robotics** drew strong technical interest as **DrJim Fan** [detailed Physical AutoResearch](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-547997fdc973) and the **ENPIRE** system's two-layer hardware safety for autonomous robotic research.
- On **AI economics**, both a researcher and **Ethan Mollick** analyzed leaked **OpenAI** financials [suggesting 40%+ serving gross margins](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-3be074716ad7) while training stays costly, linking margins to an automate-AI-research strategy.

- The **AI coding shift** resonated: **Greg Brockman** noted [software engineering is unrecognizable](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-d019681b187c) from six months ago, while a **Claude Code** contributor [framed an emerging era](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-bfb0f4650b77) where models generate correct code and humans build guardrails. **TheRundownAI** [rounded up regulation, security](/?date=2026-06-18&category=social#item-7a561a2865b8), and **Mythos/Fable** model-access tensions involving **Anthropic** and the US government.

items.title:
For years I have been saying that LLMs are not reliable enough to be sound. 

That simple claim — wh...
items.content:
For years I have been saying that LLMs are not reliable enough to be sound. 

That simple claim — which has never been refuted — is suddenly at the center of the universe. 

What Trump asks cannot be done. NO current system can completely resist circumvention.

Either we curtail LLMs until we find a better technology, or we live with consequences. 

This is not an Anthropic problem, it’s a Generative AI problem.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Following yesterday's [News](/?date=2026-06-16&category=news#item-a7b55d8864a4) coverage, Gary Marcus argues LLMs are not reliable enough to be sound, says Trump's request for non-circumventable systems cannot be met, and frames it as a generative-AI-wide problem rather than an Anthropic one.
items.reasoning: High engagement (709 likes, 56k views) tying a long-standing reliability critique to a topical policy demand; substantive and widely resonant.

For years I have been saying that LLMs are not reliable enough to be sound.

That simple claim — which has never been refuted — is suddenly at the center of the universe.

What Trump asks cannot be done. NO current system can completely resist circumvention.

Either we curtail LLMs until we find a better technology, or we live with consequences.

This is not an Anthropic problem, it’s a Generative AI problem.

items.title: I made Physical AutoResearch sound simple (conceptually), but it took a village to pull off and lots...
items.content:
I made Physical AutoResearch sound simple (conceptually), but it took a village to pull off and lots of design thinking into the robot /loopcraft. The hardest part is everything we need to setup *before* pressing Enter. Here's a behind-the-scene tour:

1. Safety harness

Letting 8 robots run unattended overnight means safety has to be more than a hint in the system prompt. ENPIRE hardwires it in 2 layers: (1) hard kinematic limit that trips an immediate task failure and auto-resets as soon as a robot leaves its safety envelope, and (2) a torque-limited compliant gripper so a bad contact or misaligned insertion ends in a safe stall, instead of crushing the robot or the object at hand. 

We make safety more conservative than usual so humans can sleep tight. In reality, we still need a few human operators to watch over the "robots of loving grace". 

2. Definition of /done

An agent that can edit its own reward will game it for sure. ENPIRE fixes the goalposts before the fleet can move them. Here's the recipe:

Collect a few minutes of success & failure demos
-> Ask agent to write code using computer vision tools to classify success and measure against groundtruth
-> Agent hill-climbs on classifier until reliably good
-> This classifier becomes the real-time reward function that directly computes on sensor streams 
-> *Freeze* the reward function before AutoResearch. It's sacred, enshrined in a Gym env that no one can touch.

3. System telemetry design

Robot-seconds is by far the scarcest resource, followed by GPU-seconds, and finally tokens. We instrument all three and surface them to ENPIRE for live resource awareness rather than letting it hill-climb in a vacuum. 

We define:
- Mean Robot Utilization ("MRU"): the fraction of wall-clock time when the robot is actively executing an experiment. Otherwise the hardware is sitting idle and waiting for the next code commit.
- Mean Token Utilization ("MTU"): tokens consumed per minute, our proxy for how hard the agent is actually thinking. A low MTU means the agent is stalled, waiting on a robot rollout to finish instead of doing research.
- GPU utilization: fraction of wall-clock time when GPU is active. 

... and evaluate on two budget-to-outcome metrics:

1. Tokens-to-Success: token budget the fleet burns to complete /goal.
2. Time-to-Success: wall-clock time to /goal

items.title: Introducing LifeSciBench, a benchmark for measuring and improving how well AI supports real-world li...
items.content:
Introducing LifeSciBench, a benchmark for measuring and improving how well AI supports real-world life science research.

Developed with 173 scientists from biotechnology and pharmaceutical research, LifeSciBench includes 750 expert-authored tasks across seven biological research workflows.

https://t.co/JTk0wXHFrT

items.title:
A roundup of the latest news surrounding Anthropic, the U.S government, and Mythos/Fable access:

- ...
items.content:
A roundup of the latest news surrounding Anthropic, the U.S government, and Mythos/Fable access:

- President Donald Trump said that talks between the two sides are 'going fine' at G7. 

- Internal messages obtained by the NYT show concern from employees that the lab is being ‘unfairly targeted’ and “bullied based on bad vibes”.

"At what point does this just feel like they don't want us to exist?"

- The Washington Post reported that the list of companies with Mythos access had recently “ballooned” from 111 to an additional 50, including a S.Korea firm with suspected ties to China.

- Trump officials told WIRED that Anthropic needs to fix the jailbreak vulnerabilities for the models to re-released.

"Security experts say that can't be done." 

- Dario Amodei, Sam Altman, Demis Hassabis, and others are currently at the G7 summit in France, expected to discuss AI regulation and safety with world leaders.

Amodei and Hassabis reportedly proposed a “U.S.-led AI coalition” at G7, with international cooperation on model access, chip exports, safety risks, and more.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: Continuing our coverage from [yesterday](/?date=2026-06-16&category=social#item-fc34249d5761), TheRundownAI compiles news on Anthropic, the US government, and Mythos/Fable model access, including G7 talks, employee fears of being unfairly targeted, an expanding access list, and demands to fix jailbreak vulnerabilities before re-release.
items.reasoning: Substantive news roundup touching regulation, model access restrictions, and security tensions involving Anthropic and government; relevant despite modest engagement.

items.content_html:
A roundup of the latest news surrounding Anthropic, the U.S government, and Mythos/Fable access:

  • President Donald Trump said that talks between the two sides are 'going fine' at G7.
  • Internal messages obtained by the NYT show concern from employees that the lab is being ‘unfairly targeted’ and “bullied based on bad vibes”.

"At what point does this just feel like they don't want us to exist?"

  • The Washington Post reported that the list of companies with Mythos access had recently “ballooned” from 111 to an additional 50, including a S.Korea firm with suspected ties to China.
  • Trump officials told WIRED that Anthropic needs to fix the jailbreak vulnerabilities for the models to re-released.

"Security experts say that can't be done."

  • Dario Amodei, Sam Altman, Demis Hassabis, and others are currently at the G7 summit in France, expected to discuss AI regulation and safety with world leaders.

Amodei and Hassabis reportedly proposed a “U.S.-led AI coalition” at G7, with international cooperation on model access, chip exports, safety risks, and more.

items.title: @rahulgs Strongly agree with all of the above. We are entering the next era of code, where the model...
items.content:
@rahulgs Strongly agree with all of the above. We are entering the next era of code, where the model is able to generate correct code for an increasingly large percent of tasks. 

Our job is to make sure the model and our systems have the right guardrails, then to run Claude Code + an advanced model + a verifier in a loop and feed it tasks (or, give the model the data it needs to generate tasks), finding and getting rid of bottlenecks along the way.
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: Claude Code contributor argues we are entering an era where models generate correct code for more tasks, with the human role shifting to building guardrails, verifiers, and running model-plus-verifier loops on tasks.
items.reasoning: Insightful, well-articulated view from a credible Claude Code engineer with high engagement; captures the shift to verifier-driven agentic coding.

items.title: Transformers keep their entire input history available and can look back into it for any fact, which...
items.content:
Transformers keep their entire input history available and can look back into it for any fact, which suits retrieval but doesn't work well with a task the authors call state tracking.

Imagine a model playing a guessing game: after each guess it says "higher" or "lower," so it must carry along the range the hidden number could still be in and narrow that range with every guess.

That range is a running summary the model has to keep and revise as the conversation goes; each guess moves it one step forward; and each narrowed range is computed from the previous range plus the latest guess, so the values form a chain, every one depending on the last.

In this recent arXiv paper from Google, the authors show transformers handle this badly: a model gives contradictory hints in this game or could flips between the two meanings of "bank" mid-conversation.

The reason is in how the information flows in a transformer. As a transformer reads input, it holds a separate vector of numbers for every word at every layer of the network, and information only ever flows upward through the layers, never back down—so once the model computes the current range and parks it at some layer, the next range, which depends on that one, can only land at the same layer or deeper, never shallower. 

Each step pushes the running summary one level up the stack, and since the stack has a fixed number of layers, a long enough conversation runs it off the top with nowhere left to put the next update.

The author argue that a proper state tracking needs recurrence—feeding a deep representation back down to a shallow one, as older recurrent networks did—and explain why this implicit recurrence beats making a model "think out loud" in extra tokens for routine bookkeeping.

https://t.co/uCmLHZxRDI

items.summary: Burkov summarizes a recent Google arXiv paper showing transformers struggle with state tracking, using a higher-or-lower guessing game and word-sense ambiguity to illustrate failure to maintain and revise a running summary as context evolves.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical explainer of a research limitation from a credible author with good engagement and shares; original educational value.

items.title: Hypesters sometimes pretend that AGI has already been achieved, but that’s not certainly true by tra...
items.content:
Hypesters sometimes pretend that AGI has already been achieved, but that’s not certainly true by traditional understandings of what that term means.

A new paper from Google DeepMind, Waterloo, ANU and UCL makes clear that what the new paper calls “competent AGI” has NOT yet been achieved, let alone what they are calling “expert” or “superhuman AGI”.

I fully concur; all claims to the contrary are just marketing.

items.title:
What "if you know what you're doing" actually looks like:

The model already has incredible general ...
items.content:
What "if you know what you're doing" actually looks like:

The model already has incredible general smarts... but what it doesn't have is what you specifically know about your field (it may know a LOT, but an expert can usually push it past what it knows pretty fast), or the exact process you'd follow (or want IT to follow, sometimes this is different, as humans and AI have different strengths).

Prompting is how you get that in. Give it your strategy, your details, your process in the right format and you go from a "good" generic answer to "wow, this is how I would've done it".

It also lets you do things neither you nor the model could've done alone.

For example, last week I worked with @PSkinnerTech on a frontend task, creating really precise/animation-heavy/complex components that we had no idea how to build on our own. But we could describe an approach: create a video of the ideal end-state, have the model attempt to build the same thing in code, write a diffing algorithm that compares its own version with the video, scores how close it is, and hill-climbs to improve.

My only reference was Levenshtein distance for text (I don't know the visual domain as well as text), and it decided on a derivative of heatmaps as a good visual equivalent. We supplied the direction and loose strategy; it supplied the hard ideas and execution.

And this isn't a one-off. It's how I built a few of my ThreeJS demos too. Being able to point at something and say "this is the quality bar," then have it loop until it hits that bar, is incredibly powerful. The model's not going to do that on its own.

You don't have to know how to do the thing, you have to know how to point at the shape of a solution.

That's prompting too, and it's more powerful than it looks.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer argues that expert prompting injects domain knowledge and process the model lacks, describing a workflow with PSkinnerTech that built complex animation-heavy frontend components via a video-target plus diffing-algorithm approach.
items.reasoning: Substantive, original take on expert-level prompting and a creative iterative workflow with good engagement and technical depth.
items.summary_html: Matt Shumer argues that expert prompting injects domain knowledge and process the model lacks, describing a workflow with PSkinnerTech that built complex animation-heavy frontend components via a video-target plus diffing-algorithm approach.
items.content_html:
What "if you know what you're doing" actually looks like:

The model already has incredible general smarts... but what it doesn't have is what you specifically know about your field (it may know a LOT, but an expert can usually push it past what it knows pretty fast), or the exact process you'd follow (or want IT to follow, sometimes this is different, as humans and AI have different strengths).

Prompting is how you get that in. Give it your strategy, your details, your process in the right format and you go from a "good" generic answer to "wow, this is how I would've done it".

It also lets you do things neither you nor the model could've done alone.

For example, last week I worked with @PSkinnerTech on a frontend task, creating really precise/animation-heavy/complex components that we had no idea how to build on our own. But we could describe an approach: create a video of the ideal end-state, have the model attempt to build the same thing in code, write a diffing algorithm that compares its own version with the video, scores how close it is, and hill-climbs to improve.

My only reference was Levenshtein distance for text (I don't know the visual domain as well as text), and it decided on a derivative of heatmaps as a good visual equivalent. We supplied the direction and loose strategy; it supplied the hard ideas and execution.

And this isn't a one-off. It's how I built a few of my ThreeJS demos too. Being able to point at something and say "this is the quality bar," then have it loop until it hits that bar, is incredibly powerful. The model's not going to do that on its own.

You don't have to know how to do the thing, you have to know how to point at the shape of a solution.

That's prompting too, and it's more powerful than it looks.

items.title: Benchmarks often test biological knowledge or narrow skills. The tasks in LifeSciBench test whether ...
items.content:
Benchmarks often test biological knowledge or narrow skills. The tasks in LifeSciBench test whether models can reason from evidence, work with scientific artifacts, handle uncertainty, and make useful decisions under real-world constraints.

GPT‑Rosalind scores above GPT‑5.5 across all seven workflows. These initial results show meaningful progress—and room to improve, particularly on artifact-heavy, design-intensive, and operationally constrained work.
items.author: @OpenAI
items.summary: OpenAI explains LifeSciBench tests reasoning from evidence and decision-making under constraints, noting GPT-Rosalind scores above GPT-5.5 across all seven workflows with room for improvement.
items.reasoning: Communicates benchmark results and relative model performance with strong reach; technically relevant to scientific AI evaluation.
items.summary_html: OpenAI explains LifeSciBench tests reasoning from evidence and decision-making under constraints, noting GPT-Rosalind scores above GPT-5.5 across all seven workflows with room for improvement.
items.content_html:
Benchmarks often test biological knowledge or narrow skills. The tasks in LifeSciBench test whether models can reason from evidence, work with scientific artifacts, handle uncertainty, and make useful decisions under real-world constraints.

GPT‑Rosalind scores above GPT‑5.5 across all seven workflows. These initial results show meaningful progress—and room to improve, particularly on artifact-heavy, design-intensive, and operationally constrained work.

items.title:
Seeing so many people saying Fable wasn’t that much better than other models.

Sure, if you’re just ...
items.content:
Seeing so many people saying Fable wasn’t that much better than other models.

Sure, if you’re just doing normal frontend/backend work that Opus/GPT 5.5 can already do, it’s going to feel basically the same.

The magic comes when you’re asking it to do things other models can’t.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer argues the Fable model only shows clear advantages on tasks other models cannot do, while feeling similar to Opus or GPT-5.5 on routine frontend/backend work. This is community discussion of the already-released Claude-Fable-5.

items.title: Maria tested the idea across 10,080 reactions, and human chemists later validated representative res...
items.content:
Maria tested the idea across 10,080 reactions, and human chemists later validated representative results by hand. 

Under the optimized conditions, yields improved for 88% of the boronic acids and 83% of the sulfonamides tested. 

Human chemists then repeated 14 representative reactions by hand: 11 showed higher yields, including 8 with a more than twofold improvement.
items.author: @OpenAI
items.summary: OpenAI reports that an AI-driven approach tested across 10,080 reactions improved yields for most boronic acids and sulfonamides, with human chemists validating representative reactions by hand.
items.reasoning: Concrete experimental results lending credibility to AI-assisted chemistry; good engagement and technical specificity.
items.summary_html: OpenAI reports that an AI-driven approach tested across 10,080 reactions improved yields for most boronic acids and sulfonamides, with human chemists validating representative reactions by hand.
items.content_html:
Maria tested the idea across 10,080 reactions, and human chemists later validated representative results by hand.

Under the optimized conditions, yields improved for 88% of the boronic acids and 83% of the sulfonamides tested.

Human chemists then repeated 14 representative reactions by hand: 11 showed higher yields, including 8 with a more than twofold improvement.

items.title: Agentic search has moved from fixed RAG pipelines into flexible agent harnesses with access to a set...
items.content:
Agentic search has moved from fixed RAG pipelines into flexible agent harnesses with access to a set of search tools: keyword search (bm25, grep regex) and semantic search.

When you upload a collection of unstructured documents to LlamaParse, we expose all these tools for agents to access.

Come check out our webinar on June 30th where we explore all these different tools and identify which ones work the best for agentic search: 

https://t.co/KrCjxY1IWV

items.summary: Jerry Liu describes the shift from fixed RAG pipelines to flexible agent harnesses with keyword and semantic search tools in LlamaParse, promoting an upcoming webinar.

items.content:
The reaction is Chan-Lam coupling, a method chemists use to build pharmaceutically relevant molecules.

One difficult version, involving primary sulfonamides, has historically produced low yields, limiting how useful it can be in medicinal chemistry.

https://t.co/RnnEiscnEg

items.title: It's actually very exciting that math is like a game. The best chess engines have an Elo of about 40...
items.content: It's actually very exciting that math is like a game. The best chess engines have an Elo of about 4000. The highest recorded human Elo is 2882, 4 stdev above an Elo of 2000 (a 99.9th percentile player). Imagine the problems we can solve when agents become as good at math.

The full process took about 2.5 months, plus another half month for human chemists to write up the results. 

This is an early example of frontier models supporting more of the scientific research loop: reviewing studies, proposing hypotheses, designing experiments, interpreting data, and surfacing findings that human experts can validate.

items.title:
My favorite models right now:

• Everyday: Claude Opus 4.7 and ChatGPT 5.5 Thinking
• Real-time news...
items.content:
My favorite models right now:

• Everyday: Claude Opus 4.7 and ChatGPT 5.5 Thinking
• Real-time news: Grok (whatever version they offer)
• Coding: Claude Code with Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.6
• Fast (running locally): Gemma 4
• Open weights: MiniMax 2.7 and Qwen 3.6

Here is something many people don't know:

You don't need a separate subscription to access all of these models.

Anuma seems to be a great one-stop shop alternative, with some cool features:

1. First, you can share the same context across models.

If you ask a question to model A, you can keep the conversation with model B without having to repeat yourself.

2. And second, you can compare answers across models, with a nice visual side-by-side comparison feature.

items.title:
We made Claude better and faster at understanding PDFs

The trick isn’t just creating the fastest fr...
items.content:
We made Claude better and faster at understanding PDFs

The trick isn’t just creating the fastest free document parser out there (with liteparse), but also *tuning the skill itself* so that Claude Code can use it with fewer turns and expensive file operations.

This is a fantastic blog post by @itsclelia which dives into the decision traces of Claude Code in how it operates over your filesystem, and identifies opportunities for optimization. We were able to incentivize the right skill behavior by doing the following:
✅ Preventing expensive mistakes like re-parsing the PDF for every search, leaving OCR on, reading screenshots when unnecessary, and preventing huge grep dumps
✅ Providing a simple BM-25 backed retrieval on parsed text
✅ Reducing the number of `grep` and `seq` sequential turns to reduce latency

The net result is that we are 37% cheaper and higher accuracy than using Claude Code over raw PDFs.

LiteParse is fully free and open-source, and you can plug in the skill today!

Blog: https://t.co/bL3EfKDpqf

Repo: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Describes optimizing a PDF-parsing skill (liteparse) so Claude Code uses fewer turns and avoids costly operations, claiming 37% cheaper and more accurate results over raw PDFs.
items.reasoning: Technically detailed, practical optimization of agentic document workflows from a credible tools founder; open-source and actionable with solid engagement.
items.summary_html: Describes optimizing a PDF-parsing skill (liteparse) so Claude Code uses fewer turns and avoids costly operations, claiming 37% cheaper and more accurate results over raw PDFs.

✅ Providing a simple BM-25 backed retrieval on parsed text

✅ Reducing the number of `grep` and `seq` sequential turns to reduce latency

The net result is that we are 37% cheaper and higher accuracy than using Claude Code over raw PDFs.

items.title:
@Curlh1 Hard agree on clear thinking, but that's kind of the point.

The model already has incredibl...
items.content:
@Curlh1 Hard agree on clear thinking, but that's kind of the point.

The model already has incredible general smarts... but what it doesn't have is what you specifically know about your field (it may know a LOT, but usually domain experts can improve that knowledge quite quickly), or the exact process you'd follow (or want IT to follow, sometimes this is different.).

Prompting is how you get that in. Give it your strategy, your details, your process in the right format and you go from a "good" generic answer to "wow, this is how I would've done it".  It also lets you do things you couldn't do yourself, or the model on its own couldn't.

Last week I worked with @PSkinnerTech on a frontend task, creating really precise/animation-heavly/complex components that we had no idea how to build on our own. But we could describe an approach: create a video of the ideal end-state, have the model attempt to build the same thing in code, write a diffing algorithm that compares its own version with the video (it decided on something close to heatmaps), scores it against the original, and hill-climbs to improve.

My only reference was Levenshtein distance for text (I don't know the visual domain as well as text), and it decided on ~heatmaps as a good visual equivalent. I supplied the direction and loose strategy; it supplied the hard ideas and execution.

You don't have to know how to do the thing, you have to know how to point at the shape of a solution. 

That's prompting too, and it's more powerful than it looks.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Matt Shumer reiterates that expert prompting supplies domain knowledge and process the model lacks, citing the same video-target plus diffing-algorithm frontend workflow with PSkinnerTech.

Whether there will be enough enterprise customers paying per token to support virtually an infinity of frontier-ish LLM providers is a rhetorical question.

items.title:
Yesterday people sure gave Snap a lot of crap about how their augmented reality glasses look.

It is...
items.content:
Yesterday people sure gave Snap a lot of crap about how their augmented reality glasses look.

It isn't how they look that will get me to buy them. I'm not gonna buy them to be fashionable.

It's what they let you do inside that matters. And that's what everyone is missing.

And that this is the worst these devices will look. In many ways these are really for developers to build the experiences that will matter once they shrink again and get cheaper.

items.title:
How it works (we’re still iterating!):

- each core teammate gets a "loop" Slack channel (ex: loop-a...
items.content:
How it works (we’re still iterating!):

- each core teammate gets a "loop" Slack channel (ex: loop-allie)
- teammates + Claude Code/Codex are in it
- anyone can drop a task in the chat
- low-risk: my AI Chief of Staff routes it, completes it, verifies, reacts ✅
- higher-risk (sending emails, pulling files): it preps but stops, drafts only
- I approve by reacting ✅
- the whole workforce runs on a 15-min loop

Essentially: teammates can prompt another teammate's AI agent workforce.

Thanks to @embirico for the Codex advice - and for accidentally inspiring this with his own Slack agent loops.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie Miller details an AI workforce system where each teammate has a Slack loop channel with Claude Code and Codex agents that route, complete, and verify low-risk tasks while drafting higher-risk ones for human approval on a 15-minute cycle.
items.reasoning: Concrete, replicable agent-orchestration workflow with practical detail, though low reach for this thread.
items.summary_html: Allie Miller details an AI workforce system where each teammate has a Slack loop channel with Claude Code and Codex agents that route, complete, and verify low-risk tasks while drafting higher-risk ones for human approval on a 15-minute cycle.
items.content_html:
How it works (we’re still iterating!):

  • each core teammate gets a "loop" Slack channel (ex: loop-allie)
  • teammates + Claude Code/Codex are in it
  • anyone can drop a task in the chat
  • low-risk: my AI Chief of Staff routes it, completes it, verifies, reacts ✅
  • higher-risk (sending emails, pulling files): it preps but stops, drafts only
  • I approve by reacting ✅
  • the whole workforce runs on a 15-min loop

Essentially: teammates can prompt another teammate's AI agent workforce.

Thanks to @embirico for the Codex advice - and for accidentally inspiring this with his own Slack agent loops.

items.title:
You know when your parents call you by your full name and you know you're in trouble? 

I just spoke...
items.content:
You know when your parents call you by your full name and you know you're in trouble? 

I just spoke with someone who uses the same trick with their AI agents. 

If they use the nickname, that means that they want their agent to chat with them more informally, easy language, funny retorts. 

If they use the government name, that means they want serious planning, formality, and bullets. 

Funny how our existing patterns can create easier agent workflows.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Original post describing how a person uses their AI agents with a name trick: a nickname signals informal, funny responses while the full government name signals serious, formal, bulleted planning.
items.reasoning: Unique, practical observation on agent UX patterns with good engagement (34 likes, 4.5K views) from a credible AI voice.
items.summary_html: Original post describing how a person uses their AI agents with a name trick: a nickname signals informal, funny responses while the full government name signals serious, formal, bulleted planning.
items.content_html:
You know when your parents call you by your full name and you know you're in trouble?

I just spoke with someone who uses the same trick with their AI agents.

If they use the nickname, that means that they want their agent to chat with them more informally, easy language, funny retorts.

If they use the government name, that means they want serious planning, formality, and bullets.

Funny how our existing patterns can create easier agent workflows.

ADIPEC 2026 is officially launched.

From 2-5 November 2026 in Abu Dhabi, the global energy community will gather to address pressure on energy systems and turn resilience, intelligence and investment into performance.

Register > https://t.co/w9vnyE9fl5

@ADIPECOfficial Partner.

Energy systems are under growing pressure.

Demand is rising, infrastructure must adapt, and resilience is becoming a practical priority for economies, industries and societies.

This is why ADIPEC 2026 matters.

Under the theme Energy. Intelligence. Impact., ADIPEC will bring together leaders from energy, technology, finance and government to strengthen energy security, improve system resilience and support long-term growth.

For me, the technology angle is especially relevant.

AI, digital infrastructure and advanced analytics are becoming part of how energy systems are monitored, optimized and protected.

The launch of ADIPEC 2026 opens an important conversation about how the global energy system can move from pressure to performance.

#ADIPEC2026 #ADIPEC #Energy #AI #DigitalTransformation

items.title: It's well known that Russian is a very difficult language to learn because of too many rules and eve...
items.content:
It's well known that Russian is a very difficult language to learn because of too many rules and even more exceptions from these rules, but even within this obvious difficulty, there's one thing that makes even me, a native speaker, crazy when I think about it.

In many languages, possessive pronouns change depending on the gender of the possessed object. For example, in French, you say "ta" (your) when the next word is feminine like "ta maison" (your house) or "ton" when the next word is masculine like "ton bateau" (your boat). Then, for "my" you would use, based on the same principle, "ma/mon" for feminine/masculine nouns and for "his/hers/its" you would use "sa/son".

In Russian, for "your" and "my," you would use "твоя" (your) or "моя" (my) for "рука" (hand) because "рука" is feminine, and you would use "мой" and "твой" for "дом" (house) because "дом" is masculine, BUT you would use "его" (his) for nouns of any gender when the possessor is male, and you would use "её" (her) for nouns of any gender because the possessor is female.

So, as a foreign language learner, you must not only remember by heart the gender of each noun (which is often different from genders of your language), but you must also learn to ignore this detail when using nouns with a possessive pronoun in the third person only, and, instead, keep track of the gender of the person possessing the noun like you do in English! 🤯
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov writes a detailed linguistics observation about the difficulty of Russian possessive pronouns compared to French, focusing on gender-of-possessor versus gender-of-object rules.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful but largely off-topic linguistics content from an ML author; only tangentially relevant to NLP.
items.summary_html: Andriy Burkov writes a detailed linguistics observation about the difficulty of Russian possessive pronouns compared to French, focusing on gender-of-possessor versus gender-of-object rules.
items.content_html:
It's well known that Russian is a very difficult language to learn because of too many rules and even more exceptions from these rules, but even within this obvious difficulty, there's one thing that makes even me, a native speaker, crazy when I think about it.

In many languages, possessive pronouns change depending on the gender of the possessed object. For example, in French, you say "ta" (your) when the next word is feminine like "ta maison" (your house) or "ton" when the next word is masculine like "ton bateau" (your boat). Then, for "my" you would use, based on the same principle, "ma/mon" for feminine/masculine nouns and for "his/hers/its" you would use "sa/son".

In Russian, for "your" and "my," you would use "твоя" (your) or "моя" (my) for "рука" (hand) because "рука" is feminine, and you would use "мой" and "твой" for "дом" (house) because "дом" is masculine, BUT you would use "его" (his) for nouns of any gender when the possessor is male, and you would use "её" (her) for nouns of any gender because the possessor is female.

So, as a foreign language learner, you must not only remember by heart the gender of each noun (which is often different from genders of your language), but you must also learn to ignore this detail when using nouns with a possessive pronoun in the third person only, and, instead, keep track of the gender of the person possessing the noun like you do in English! 🤯

items.title:
The enterprise takeaway is simple:

AI performance is now a system-level challenge.

The winners wil...
items.content:
The enterprise takeaway is simple:

AI performance is now a system-level challenge.

The winners will optimize chips, memory, interconnects, software, and architecture together.

Less latency means faster intelligence.
Less movement means lower cost.
Less waste means AI that can scale.

Read the full Article https://t.co/1AaYJV6e9z

Where do you think the biggest AI infrastructure gains will come from next, smaller chips, better systems, or something else entirely?

#HuaweiPartner @huawei  #Huawei
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Concludes a Huawei-partner thread arguing AI performance is a system-level challenge spanning chips, memory, interconnects, and software.

items.title:
AI’s next bottleneck is not just compute.

It is time.

Time lost moving data.
Time lost coordinatin...
items.content:
AI’s next bottleneck is not just compute.

It is time.

Time lost moving data.
Time lost coordinating chips.
Time lost waiting on memory, interconnects, and software layers to catch up.

That shift changes how we should think about AI infrastructure.

A thread…

#HuaweiPartner @huawei

items.title:
@shyamvaran Come to San Francisco and start a company. Many have their hands out to them. 

Big comp...
items.content:
@shyamvaran Come to San Francisco and start a company. Many have their hands out to them. 

Big companies are laying people off. They won't hire newbies who need to be trained unless they are entrepreneurial too. And most aren't.

AI is also doing the shit jobs that new graduates used to do. 

So college students need to learn skills that are above that. Some are. But most aren't.

And most students aren't really using AI to build things. Which is what's in demand.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Reply advising students to move to San Francisco and be entrepreneurial, arguing AI is taking the entry-level jobs grads used to do, so students must learn higher-level skills and actually build with AI.
items.reasoning: Substantive opinion on AI's effect on entry-level work and the skills gap, with moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Reply advising students to move to San Francisco and be entrepreneurial, arguing AI is taking the entry-level jobs grads used to do, so students must learn higher-level skills and actually build with AI.
items.content_html:
@shyamvaran Come to San Francisco and start a company. Many have their hands out to them.

Big companies are laying people off. They won't hire newbies who need to be trained unless they are entrepreneurial too. And most aren't.

AI is also doing the shit jobs that new graduates used to do.

So college students need to learn skills that are above that. Some are. But most aren't.

And most students aren't really using AI to build things. Which is what's in demand.

items.title:
What? Oh shit. 

He was so awesome and a tireless community builder and company builder. 

Devastati...
items.content:
What? Oh shit. 

He was so awesome and a tireless community builder and company builder. 

Devastating for the Austin startup community. 

I have so many memories of him, having attended SXSW as a speaker something like 25 years and working for Rackspace, which was an hour away and frequented his events because of that.

Huge loss for Texas and America.

Huge loss for Texas and America.

items.title:
One concept that makes this practical is LogicFolding.

Traditional chips spread logic across a flat...
items.content:
One concept that makes this practical is LogicFolding.

Traditional chips spread logic across a flat surface.

LogicFolding brings related logic closer together by moving toward more 3D structures.

Less distance means less delay.

And in AI workloads, small delays compound fast.

items.title:
For decades, the industry had a clear playbook:

→ Shrink transistors
→ Increase density
→ Improve p...
items.content:
For decades, the industry had a clear playbook:

→ Shrink transistors
→ Increase density
→ Improve performance
→ Lower cost

Moore’s Law powered the modern computing era.

But that curve is slowing.

The next optimization target is not only geometry.

It is latency.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Frames latency rather than transistor geometry as the next optimization target beyond Moore's Law.

items.title:
I've been trying Retool for quite a while now. Here is the link to their platform:

https://t.co/wA5...
items.content:
I've been trying Retool for quite a while now. Here is the link to their platform:

https://t.co/wA54iOax88

Retool lets you do something nobody else does:

1. You can keep vibe-coding with Claude, Codex, etc.
2. You can import the app into Retool
3. Your app will inherit everything your company runs on

You are basically getting all of the following:

• Database credentials
• Single Sign-On
• Permissions
• Audit logs

Imagine running 3 separate vibe-coded apps and handling permissions and database access on every one of them. This is a nightmare.

Instead, you import your apps into Retool, and now you have a central place to manage everything.

This is a pretty awesome idea.

Thanks to the Retool team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title: This is why many manufacturers are rethinking where AI decisions happen, not just how good those mod...
items.content:
This is why many manufacturers are rethinking where AI decisions happen, not just how good those models are.
 
The shift is moving decision-making to the edge of the network, inside the factory itself.
 
That means:
→ No dependency on distant cloud infrastructure
→ Ultra-low latency for automated systems
→ Time-sensitive quality control that actually keeps pace with the line
→ Tighter automation loops and higher production precision
 
Processing decisions closer to operations can help reduce delays in automated workflows.
 
Autonomous manufacturing depends on responsive, low-latency decision-making.

items.title:
Here’s how the hidden latency problem works.
 
A production line runs 24/7. Cameras inspect products...
items.content:
Here’s how the hidden latency problem works.
 
A production line runs 24/7. Cameras inspect products in low-latency production environments. AI detects defects.
 
But if that decision happens in the cloud, here’s what occurs:
→ Data leaves the factory → Gets sent to a remote server → Gets processed → Instructions come back
 
And while all of that is happening, the line keeps moving.
More defective products pass through. More material gets wasted. More rework piles up.
 
The AI model may perform accurately, but delayed response times can still impact operations.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Explains the hidden latency problem of cloud-based defect detection on a continuous production line.
items.reasoning: Decent illustrative framing of edge latency but promotional and low engagement.
items.summary_html: Explains the hidden latency problem of cloud-based defect detection on a continuous production line.
items.content_html:

But if that decision happens in the cloud, here’s what occurs:

→ Data leaves the factory → Gets sent to a remote server → Gets processed → Instructions come back

And while all of that is happening, the line keeps moving.

More defective products pass through. More material gets wasted. More rework piles up.

The AI model may perform accurately, but delayed response times can still impact operations.

items.title:
This is the idea behind Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers as Her’s Law.

Instead of asking onl...
items.content:
This is the idea behind Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers as Her’s Law.

Instead of asking only, “How small can the chip get?”

We now have to ask:

→ Where is time being lost?
→ Where is data waiting?
→ Where are signals traveling too far?
→ Where is the system creating friction?

items.title:
You have about 50x to 100x higher odds to die in a private jet than flying commercial

The reason is...
items.content:
You have about 50x to 100x higher odds to die in a private jet than flying commercial

The reason isn't that the planes are bad btw, they're great, but private jets flights are much less strictly regulated than commercial flights

They can fly more custom routes, fly into tiny airports and when the passenger (usually a rich person) really needs to be at a meeting in time they might decide to fly through bad weather when a commercial flight would cancel or delay the flight

Simply speaking private jets have a lot more freedom which is good and bad

Commercial flights are extremely strictly regulated, fly extremely boring routes, the same every day or every few hours back and forth, highly predictable and if one pilot encounters some issue like bad weather they're able to tell the next flying the same route

Even if I could afford to fly private, I'd prefer to just board with fast track and go first or business class, it's much safer!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio explains why private jets carry far higher per-trip fatality odds than commercial flights, attributing it to lighter regulation and riskier routing rather than poor aircraft.
items.reasoning: Detailed and high-engagement, but entirely off-topic from AI.
items.summary_html: Levelsio explains why private jets carry far higher per-trip fatality odds than commercial flights, attributing it to lighter regulation and riskier routing rather than poor aircraft.

You have about 50x to 100x higher odds to die in a private jet than flying commercial

They can fly more custom routes, fly into tiny airports and when the passenger (usually a rich person) really needs to be at a meeting in time they might decide to fly through bad weather when a commercial flight would cancel or delay the flight

Simply speaking private jets have a lot more freedom which is good and bad

Commercial flights are extremely strictly regulated, fly extremely boring routes, the same every day or every few hours back and forth, highly predictable and if one pilot encounters some issue like bad weather they're able to tell the next flying the same route

Even if I could afford to fly private, I'd prefer to just board with fast track and go first or business class, it's much safer!

items.content:
True. 

Gotta see a path to getting consumers, even someday in the future. I don't see how Snap gets there. And Meta is a mess internally. 

Which is why I'm betting on Google and Apple and watching a range of them coming out of China, where consumers are far more excited by all this stuff than elsewhere.

items.content:
This is exactly what Edge Control from @TMobileBusiness is designed to support.
 
By routing data closer to where it is generated, Edge Control helps deliver ultra-low latency, local data processing, and private-network-like performance without the complexity of building a traditional private network.
 
Deployments can also be managed through T-Platform, T-Mobile’s unified portal for monitoring advanced network solutions.
 
It’s a managed solution built for high-demand, low-latency operational environments.

Mo...
items.content:
@pkblather @t_blom I drive hours every week. You could not be more wrong. FSD is a life changer.

Most people THINK they love to drive. OK, maybe on a race track or a rare curvy road with no traffic. 

Even there FSD is far superior. Why?

You can enjoy the scenery, or take a sip of coffee, or talk to someone else who is in the car with you.

https://t.co/9M2ZWIs83F

items.content:
@pkblather @t_blom I totally disagree. 

My wife will tell you how big a deal it is.

But it will be obvious over the next year when it turns unsupervised and lets you sit in the passenger seat as it drives.

And Cybercab is coming that doesn't even have a steering wheel. 

I'm buying one.

@pkblather @t_blom I used to drive a lot to Las Vegas. An eight hour drive for me. 

I could only ge...
items.content:
@pkblather @t_blom I used to drive a lot to Las Vegas. An eight hour drive for me. 

I could only get half way before I'd have to pull over and take a rest.

I no longer do.

And I don't think you have FSD 4 with latest builds.

It works just fine in San Francisco, which is just as chaotic and impatient as London.

https://t.co/9M2ZWIs83F
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Reply recounting how FSD handles long drives and works in chaotic San Francisco traffic, suggesting the interlocutor lacks the latest build.
items.reasoning: Anecdotal FSD endorsement with low engagement.

items.content_html:
@pkblather @t_blom I used to drive a lot to Las Vegas. An eight hour drive for me.

I could only get half way before I'd have to pull over and take a rest.

I no longer do.

And I don't think you have FSD 4 with latest builds.

It works just fine in San Francisco, which is just as chaotic and impatient as London.

items.title:
Apple warning people "your privacy is at risk."

I love it anyway even knowing all this. And I knew ...
items.content:
Apple warning people "your privacy is at risk."

I love it anyway even knowing all this. And I knew it before I loaded it. It watches everything on your screen and you give it access to everything if you do load it. 

There's got to be a better way to warn consumers that they are about to give a company everything. It isn't alone. I have several running that also have access to everything. 

Drives security professionals nuts. But brings deep utility to users.

In manufacturing, milliseconds used to be a technical metric.
Now they’re becoming an operational consideration.
 
The companies treating latency as a competitive lever are the ones improving precision, efficiency, and automation reliability.
 
Watch the full video to see how manufacturers are redesigning low-latency factory operations with Edge Control from @TMobileBusiness.
 
Learn more: https://t.co/7BLgoYkULw 
 
What’s the biggest bottleneck you’re seeing in manufacturing AI deployments right now: the models, the infrastructure, or the integration?

Mostly it's sunny here when we're usually using power, and then our cars charge in the middle of the night or during the day. So everything's good.
